lessonTitle: Part-Time Work, Work-Study, Side Hustles, and Entrepreneurship
concept: Earning money at university is possible — but the rules, limits, and trade-offs depend heavily on your visa status and how much your studies actually demand.

hook.fact: Marcus earns €500/month from a campus job. Kai earns €0 because he assumed his visa didn't allow it.
hook.question: Kai assumed wrong.

conceptCards.title: Card 1
conceptCards.body: Most student visas permit some paid work — but with limits. UK Tier 4 students can work up to 20 hours/week during term. US F-1 students can work on-campus up to 20 hours/week during term; off-campus work requires special authorisation.

conceptCards.title: Card 2
conceptCards.body: **Work-study programmes** (common in the US) are government-funded arrangements where eligible students work part-time on campus. The jobs are designed around academic schedules and often pay above minimum wage. Availability depends on financial need and is allocated through the FAFSA

conceptCards.title: Card 3
conceptCards.body: Campus jobs — library assistants, lab support, sports coaching, tutoring — are worth prioritising over off-campus work. They are shorter commutes, more flexible around exam periods, and employers understand academic commitments better.

conceptCards.title: Card 4
conceptCards.body: Freelancing and gig economy work (tutoring, translation, design, copywriting, delivery) offer flexible income but less predictability. Tax obligations on freelance income vary by country — in the UK, earnings above £1,000/year from self-employment must be declared via Self Assessment. In

conceptCards.title: Card 5
conceptCards.body: The real cost of part-time work is time. 10 hours/week = 400+ hours/year. Research consistently shows that more than 15–20 hours/week of paid work correlates with lower grades. The financial return must be worth the academic cost.

conceptCards.title: Card 6
conceptCards.body: Now test this: Student entrepreneurship — building a small product, service, or online business — can generate income while building skills. However, it carries zero guarantee of income and high time demands. For funding reliability, it is a supplement, not

questions.prompt: How many hours per week can a UK Tier 4 student typically work during term time?
questions.explanation: "UK Tier 4 (now called Student visa) permits up to 20 hours/week during term time. Exceeding this is a visa violation."

questions.statement: US F-1 students are free to work off-campus at any job during their studies.
questions.whyPrompt: Why is that true or false?
questions.explanation: "F-1 students can work on-campus up to 20 hours/week during term without special permission. Off-campus employment requires specific authorisation (CPT or OPT) and is not freely available."

questions.prompt: Match each income type to its primary advantage:
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: "Research consistently shows that working more than ___ hours/week correlates with lower academic grades."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.prompt: Marcus works 12 hours/week at €12/hour during a 30-week academic year.
questions.explanation: "12 × €12 = €144/week. €144 × 30 weeks = €4,320/year — a meaningful contribution to his €8,000 annual funding gap."

questions.sentence: Complete: "Before accepting 25 hours/week of paid work, a student should check whether their ___ permits it, whether it exceeds the ___ hour threshold linked to grade decline, and whether the financial gain is worth the ___ cost."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.scenario: Kai's plan: "I'll start an e-commerce business during my first year and use the profits to fund my studies."
options: Entrepreneurial income is unpredictable — using it as a primary funding source
Kai would need a separate business visa
questions.explanation: "Entrepreneurship is a supplement, not a substitute. Most first-year student businesses generate little or no income in Year 1. Treating unpredictable income as a funding plan is how students end up in financial crisis."

tasks.scenario: Priya has a €6,000 annual funding gap. She is considering:
tasks.explanation: "Option B pays more but risks visa violations (20 hours is the maximum, any overage is a breach), takes time away from studies year-round, and delivery work may fall outside approved categories. Option A is safer and still covers most of the gap."

tasks.scenario: Sofia works 15 hours/week during 30 weeks of term at S$14/hour.
tasks.prompt: What percentage does her income represent?
tasks.explanation: "15 × S$14 × 30 = S$6,300/year. S$6,300 ÷ S$12,000 = 52.5%. Part-time work can cover a majority of living costs — but not all. Sofia still needs a plan for the other 47.5%."

lockIn.takeaway: Earning while studying is feasible and worthwhile — but always verify your visa's specific work limits before accepting any job.

reviewQueueEntry.definition: Visa work restrictions anchors better education money decisions.
reviewQueueEntry.quickRecallPrompt: What does Visa work restrictions include beyond tuition?
